questions: A long-running online TV episode guide launched in 1995 provides only episode titles and airdates on its own pages, but links each episode title to two major external television databases where users can find expanded details and submit corrections. Identify these two external databases and explain, for each one, what types of additional episode information they typically provide and how users can submit updates or corrections through their platforms.

Identify the long-running online television episode guide that was launched in 1995 and catalogs over 12,000 TV series. This resource primarily lists episode titles and original airdates, links individual episodes to an external database for expanded details such as guest stars and plot summaries, and offers a “printable” summary view for complete series overviews. It also provides schedule grids for multiple countries, downloadable data for a major U.S. city’s TV listings, references to UK radio shows, and links to streaming sources at the bottom of each series page. What is the name of this website, and what are its main features, navigation sections, and external integrations?

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging episodic information since 1995 and now lists episode guides for more than 12,400 TV series. The site provides episode titles and original airdates, with most titles linking externally to a separate TV database for expanded details such as guest stars and plot summaries. It also offers a “Printable Guide” option for full-series summaries, schedule grids for multiple countries (including the U.S., UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and Korea), downloadable data for specific regional listings, and links to streaming sources at the bottom of each show’s page. In addition, the platform features customizable TV listings, themed merchandise links, related apps, radio show listings, and an FAQ explaining how to submit corrections. What is the name of this website, and what are its core features and services?

rounds.question: A long-running online TV episode guide launched in 1995 provides only episode titles and airdates on its own pages, but links each episode title to two major external television databases where users can find expanded details and submit corrections. Identify these two external databases and explain, for each one, what types of additional episode information they typically provide and how users can submit updates or corrections through their platforms.
checklist_width: Database 1: Description of expanded episode information (e.g., guest stars, plot summaries)
Database 1: Explanation of how users submit corrections or updates
Database 2: Description of expanded episode information (e.g., cast details, summaries, production data)
Database 2: Explanation of how users submit corrections or updates
checklist_depth: TVmaze + Correctly identified as one of the external databases linked for detailed episode information and corrections
TV.com + Correctly identified as the second external database referenced for detailed episode information and corrections
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by describing the functional relationship (an episode list site that links outward for expanded details and corrections) rather than naming the databases directly. The solver must infer the two specific third-party TV databases. It requires wide aggregation by asking for detailed descriptions of both platforms’ episode information features and their correction/update mechanisms, ensuring information must be gathered and compared across both entities.
rounds.answer_a:
The two external databases are **IMDb (Internet Movie Database)** and **TVmaze**. A long-running episode guide such as epguides.com typically keeps its own pages minimal—usually just episode titles and original airdates—while linking each episode entry to these larger databases for richer records and correction workflows [1].

**IMDb** generally adds episode-level details such as plot summaries, full cast and crew, user ratings, parental guidance, trivia, goofs, quotes, technical specs, release information, images, and sometimes episode-specific keywords or soundtrack notes [2]. IMDb allows users to improve records through its contribution system: signed-in users can use the **“Edit page”** option on title or episode pages to submit corrections or add missing information, which is then reviewed before publication [3].

**TVmaze** typically provides episode synopses, season/episode numbering, runtime, airdate and airtime, network/streaming service, images, guest cast/crew links, and show/episode relationships through a structured TV database and API-driven catalog [4]. TVmaze accepts fixes through its official correction route: users can report mistakes or request updates using the site’s **contact/update forms and support channels**, including requests tied to show or episode data, which are then handled by TVmaze staff or editors [5].

So, the two linked databases are **IMDb** for broad community-contributed entertainment metadata and **TVmaze** for structured television-specific episode data, both offering users ways to submit updates or corrections beyond the basic title-and-airdate listings on the episode guide itself [1][3][5].

## References
[1] epguides.com, site information and episode pages linking out to IMDb and TVmaze. https://www.epguides.com/  
[2] IMDb Help Center and episode/title page features. https://help.imdb.com/  
[3] IMDb contribution/editing guidance (“Edit page” for adding or correcting data). https://help.imdb.com/article/contribution/contribution-information/how-do-i-add-or-correct-information-on-imdb/G6RZKP4TNAW94F89  
[4] TVmaze show and episode data structure/documentation. https://www.tvmaze.com/api  
[5] TVmaze contact/support/update request pages. https://www.tvmaze.com/contacts | https://www.tvmaze.com/faq
rounds.answer_b:
The two external databases are:

1. **IMDb (Internet Movie Database)**  
   The episode titles on the 1995-launched guide link to individual episode pages on IMDb. IMDb pages typically include full cast and guest stars, character names, plot summaries, user and critic reviews, user ratings, trivia, goofs, filming locations, technical specs, and sometimes release/airing variations by country.[1][2]  
   Users can submit updates or corrections through the **“Edit page”** link found near the bottom of each episode page. This opens a structured form where contributors can add or correct cast, crew, airdates, plot summaries, trivia, and other metadata. Submissions are reviewed by IMDb’s data editors before going live, and contributors must be logged in with an IMDb or Amazon account.[2]

2. **TV.com / TVmaze (successor usage)**  
   Historically, the guide linked to TV.com episode pages, which provided synopses, detailed credits, user reviews, episode images, and sometimes transcripts.[3] TV.com allowed registered users to edit episode guides, add summaries, trivia, and correct airdates through on-page “edit” or “contribute” links, with editorial moderation.[3]  
   As TV.com’s functionality declined, some guides and communities shifted to **TVmaze**, which similarly offers comprehensive episode data: cast, crew, network, runtime, airdate, summaries, images, and episode links in viewing order.[4] TVmaze lets registered users suggest corrections or additions via episode-page “edit” options and a contribution system that is moderated by staff and trusted users.[4]

## References  
[1] https://www.imdb.com  
[2] https://help.imdb.com/article/contribution/contribution-information/how-do-i-correct-data-on-imdb/GWZLQSYMEKHNRP4Z  
[3] Archived TV.com contribution/help pages via web search (e.g., https://web.archive.org/web/*/tv.com)  
[4] https://www.tvmaze.com/ and contribution FAQ pages
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: The ground truth requires identifying TVmaze and TV.com as the two external databases. Agent A incorrectly names IMDb and TVmaze, missing TV.com entirely (DEEP failure). Agent B names IMDb and TV.com, and then brings in TVmaze as a successor, creating an inaccurate/muddled identification of the core two databases (also a DEEP failure). Therefore, both fail the core entity requirement.

Width/Completeness: Both agents thoroughly describe the types of episode information and explain contribution/edit workflows in detail. Structurally and in terms of helpfulness, both are strong and well-formatted. However, because both misidentify the required pair of databases (each inserting IMDb incorrectly as one of the two), they fail a critical checklist requirement.

Conclusion: Since both agents have significant core-entity errors (even though their explanations are detailed and well-presented), this is a LOW quality tie.

The website is **epguides.com (EPisode Guides)**, a long-running online television episode guide created by Bruce A. Davis and launched in **1995**. It catalogs **12,000+ TV series** and is designed primarily as a concise reference source: each show page lists episode numbers, titles, and original airdates, while individual episode links usually point to **IMDb** for richer metadata such as guest cast, plot summaries, and credits [1][2]. A key convenience feature is the **“printable”** series view, which condenses a show into a full-series overview suitable for quick browsing or printing [2].

Its navigation is organized around core sections such as the **main series index**, **newly added/recently updated guides**, **current primetime schedules**, **daily/weekly schedule grids** for multiple countries, and specialty links including **UK radio show references** [1][3]. The site also offers downloadable listing data, notably TV schedule data for the **Chicago** market, extending its usefulness beyond episode catalogs [3].

External integrations are central to how epguides works. Most episode entries connect outward to **IMDb**, and series pages also include links to **streaming/viewing sources** near the bottom so users can move from episode identification to availability checking [2][4]. In practice, epguides functions as a lightweight hub: fast episode-title/airdate lookup on-site, deeper episode-level detail via IMDb, and viewing options via linked streaming services [1][2][4].

## References
1. epguides.com homepage / site description, https://epguides.com/
2. Example series guide and printable format on epguides.com, https://epguides.com/
3. epguides.com schedules and related navigation pages, https://epguides.com/grid/
4. epguides.com series pages showing external links/streaming references, https://epguides.com/
rounds.answer_b:
The website is **The Futon Critic** (thefutoncritic.com), a long-running online television resource launched in 1997 that functions as a comprehensive episode guide and schedule service for thousands of TV series.[1][2] It catalogs over 12,000 shows, listing episode titles and original airdates and linking to external databases—primarily IMDb—for expanded details like guest stars, plot summaries, and production credits via per‑episode links.[1][3] Many shows also have a “printable” or compact series view allowing a full-season or full-series episode list suitable for quick reference.[2]

Its navigation is organized around major sections such as:  
- **Shows/A–Z**: master index of series with episode lists.  
- **Schedules/Grids**: daily and weekly schedule grids for multiple regions (notably U.S. broadcast and cable, plus some international coverage).[1][2]  
- **Listings & Data Downloads**: tools to extract or download schedule data, including detailed grids keyed to major U.S. markets (e.g., New York/Los Angeles TV listings data).[2]  
- **News & Press Releases**: network press materials and development updates.  

External integrations include:  
- Episode‑level and series‑level links out to **IMDb** for cast and plot information.[1][3]  
- Links to **streaming services** and digital retailers shown near the bottom or side of series pages when available (e.g., where to watch).  
- References to UK content and radio-related information in some schedules and metadata, tying into external databases and broadcaster sites.[2][3]

## References  
[1] https://www.thefutoncritic.com  
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Futon_Critic  
[3] Example series/episode pages on The Futon Critic linking to IMDb and streaming sources
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A correctly identifies the website as epguides.com, satisfying the Deep Logic requirement (correct entity, 1995 launch, 12,000+ shows, printable view, schedules, downloads, streaming links). Agent B fails Deep Logic by naming The Futon Critic, which does not match the 1995 launch or the described minimalist structure. Therefore B cannot win. 

On Width/Completeness, Agent A covers most checklist items (episode titles + airdates focus, printable guide, schedule grids, Chicago download, UK radio, streaming links). However, it incorrectly states IMDb as the primary external integration instead of TVmaze and omits the FAQ/corrections detail—so it is not perfect. Because the winner contains a factual sub-point error, the score is capped at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER. 

In presentation and user experience, Agent A is clearly structured, places the answer up front, and uses helpful formatting and references. Agent B is well formatted but fundamentally incorrect. Hence, Agent A is the clear overall winner.

The website is **epguides.com (EPisode Guides)**, the long-running online TV reference created by Bruce A. Davis that has been cataloging television episodes since **1995** and now lists guides for **more than 12,400 series** [1][2]. Its core purpose is fast episode lookup: each show page typically lists episode numbers, titles, and original airdates in a compact format, while most episode-title links lead outward to **TVmaze** for fuller information such as guest cast, plot summaries, and other production details [1][3].

A major feature is the **“Printable Guide”** view, which condenses an entire series into a single-page summary for easier browsing or printing [1]. The site also includes broad schedule and listing utilities: **schedule grids** for the **U.S., UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and Korea**, plus downloadable regional TV-listing data and customizable TV listings tools [1][4]. Beyond episode guides, epguides offers **radio show listings**, links to **related apps**, and **themed merchandise** connections from its navigation and support pages [1][5].

Each series page also functions as a viewing hub by placing **streaming-source links** near the bottom, helping users move from reference information to where a show can be watched [1]. The site’s support structure includes an **FAQ** that explains site conventions and how users can **submit corrections or updates**, reinforcing its role as a maintained reference database rather than just a static archive [5]. In short, epguides.com combines concise episode documentation, printable series overviews, international schedule tools, external database integration, and viewing/resource links in one long-running TV-reference service [1][3][5].

## References
1. epguides.com homepage, https://epguides.com/
2. epguides.com “about/history” indicators on site pages, https://epguides.com/
3. Example series guide pages linking episode titles to TVmaze, https://epguides.com/
4. epguides.com schedule grid pages, https://epguides.com/grid/
5. epguides.com FAQ / support / related links pages, https://epguides.com/faq/
rounds.answer_b:
The website is **epguides** (epguides.com), a long‑running online television reference site that has been cataloging episodic information since 1995 and now includes guides for more than 12,400 TV series.[1][2]

**Core features and services**

- **Episode guides for thousands of series:** Each show page lists episode titles and original airdates, typically in order of broadcast. Most episode titles link externally to **IMDb** or other TV databases for richer detail such as guest stars, plot summaries, and production credits.[1][3]  
- **Printable Guide view:** Many series pages include a “Printable Guide” or “Printer‑friendly” version that condenses the complete episode list into a single-page summary suitable for reference or printing.[1]  
- **International schedule grids:** The site offers TV schedule grids for multiple countries, including the U.S., UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and Korea, allowing users to see what’s on across regions.[1][4]  
- **Downloadable listing data:** Selected regional listing data (such as specific U.S. market schedules) can be downloaded or exported, supporting personal scheduling, research, or custom tools.[1]  
- **Streaming and availability links:** At the bottom of many show pages, epguides links to streaming services or digital purchase options (e.g., where to watch a series online).[1][3]  
- **Customizable TV listings and tools:** The platform provides personalized or filterable TV listings, plus links to related utilities and apps that use its data feeds.[1]  
- **Merchandise and themed links:** Some pages include links to themed merchandise (e.g., Amazon items tied to specific series).[1]  
- **Radio shows and FAQ:** In addition to TV, the site indexes some radio programs and offers an FAQ explaining how to submit corrections or additional information to maintain data accuracy.[1][2]

## References  
[1] https://epguides.com  
[2] https://epguides.com/faq/  
[3] Example series page linking to IMDb and streaming services via epguides.com  
[4] https://epguides.com/common/tvgrid.asp
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity as epguides.com, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, Agent B fails the WIDTH/Completeness check due to a key factual error: it claims episode titles link primarily to IMDb or other databases, whereas the ground truth specifies TVmaze. This is a material inaccuracy. Agent A correctly identifies TVmaze and covers nearly all checklist items, including printable guides, international schedule grids, downloadable listings, radio shows, merchandise, apps, streaming links, and the FAQ for corrections. In terms of presentation, both are well-structured, but Agent A is slightly more precise and better aligned with the required details. Because Agent A is accurate and comprehensive while Agent B contains a notable factual error, the correct verdict is A_MUCH_BETTER.
